principle: A single-color or tonal outfit spanning head to toe with minimal interruption creates an unbroken vertical visual path that may elongate the perceived silhouette.
observable_patterns: continuous dark or light region from shoulder to hem
minimal tonal contrast at waist or hip transition
matching or near-matching top and bottom hues
no strong horizontal color break at any garment boundary
possible_effects: increases perceived vertical length of the figure
reduces visible segmentation between garment zones
creates a unified silhouette impression
may reduce perceived width relative to height
limitations: lighting conditions alter tonal relationships between garments
fabric texture differences may reintroduce visible segmentation despite color match
camera angle and cropping affect perceived elongation
accessories or footwear breaking the color path may interrupt continuity
example_claims: The outfit creates strong vertical continuity through matched tonal values.
No dominant horizontal color break is detected between garment zones.
The silhouette reads as a single uninterrupted vertical region.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style
Trinny and Susannah body proportion framework

principle: A strong tonal or structural contrast at the waist zone visually divides the figure into upper and lower segments, anchoring the eye at the waist boundary.
observable_patterns: high contrast between top and bottom garment colors at waist
belt or waistband with distinct color difference from surrounding fabric
tucked-in top creating a visible fabric edge at waist
color block boundary coinciding with natural or apparent waist zone
possible_effects: draws focal attention to the waist region
creates visible horizontal division of the silhouette
may shorten the perceived vertical length of each segment
anchors perceived proportional relationship between upper and lower body
limitations: waist zone visibility depends on garment fit and tucking
loose or untucked layers may obscure waist boundary
pose and posture affect where the apparent waist falls
wide-angle distortion may misplace apparent waist position
example_claims: A high-contrast boundary is visible at the waist, creating horizontal segmentation.
The silhouette appears divided into two distinct vertical segments.
Waist emphasis is created by a visible tonal contrast at the midsection.
source_refs: Trinny and Susannah body shape framework
Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: The apparent width of the shoulder line relative to the hip line determines whether the overall silhouette reads as balanced, top-heavy, or bottom-heavy.
observable_patterns: padded or structured shoulder seams extending beyond natural shoulder edge
wide lapels or horizontal collar elements broadening the shoulder visual
A-line or flared lower garment increasing hip/hem width
narrow or draped shoulder line with volume concentrated lower
shoulder seam sitting on or near the crest of the shoulder
possible_effects: broad shoulder line may increase perceived upper body visual weight
narrow shoulder line relative to wider lower garment may suggest bottom-heavy silhouette
matched shoulder and hip width may create an hourglass or rectangular silhouette impression
structured shoulders suggest tailored or formal visual register
limitations: camera perspective may exaggerate or reduce apparent shoulder width
pose angle affects shoulder-to-hip ratio reading
layering above or below may mask structural shoulder line
fabric stiffness affects how far the shoulder silhouette extends
example_claims: Structured shoulder line appears wider than the lower garment, suggesting a top-heavy silhouette.
The flared lower garment creates greater visual width at the hem than at the shoulder.
Shoulder seam placement appears at or beyond the natural shoulder crest.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Vertical design lines—including seams, plackets, stripes, buttons, or garment edges—running parallel to the body axis may increase perceived vertical length within that zone.
observable_patterns: vertical seam lines visible on garment surface
vertical stripe pattern on fabric
long row of buttons or zipper running top to bottom
open layering piece creating two parallel vertical edges
V-neck or deep neckline creating a downward-pointing angle
possible_effects: may increase perceived vertical length in the garment zone
reduces horizontal visual weight in stripe-bearing zone
creates directional eye movement downward or upward along garment
V-neck angle draws eye inward and downward, lengthening neck and chest zone
limitations: stripe width affects whether elongation or width perception dominates
print scale and spacing relative to figure scale alters perception
competing horizontal elements may cancel vertical line effect
garment length determines extent of vertical line travel
example_claims: Vertical seam lines are visible running through the torso zone.
A V-neck creates a downward-pointing visual line from collar to chest.
The open cardigan creates two parallel vertical edges framing the front.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Horizontal design elements—stripes, seams, hems, wide belts, color blocks—may increase perceived width in the zones where they appear.
observable_patterns: horizontal stripe pattern on fabric
wide horizontal belt or waistband
boat neck or horizontal collar line
color block with horizontal boundary across garment
horizontal pocket placement at hip or chest
possible_effects: may increase perceived horizontal width in the zone containing the horizontal element
anchors visual attention at the horizontal element boundary
can reduce apparent vertical length by segmenting the region
boat neckline may broaden the apparent shoulder span
limitations: stripe scale and spacing interact with figure scale
pose and camera angle affect how horizontal reads versus diagonal
multiple competing horizontal elements may diffuse the width effect
tight or fitted garments diminish the width-adding effect of horizontal lines
example_claims: A horizontal stripe pattern is present in the upper torso zone.
The wide belt creates a prominent horizontal boundary at the waist.
The boat neckline creates a horizontal visual line at the shoulder level.

principle: Garments with fit aligned close to the body surface produce cleaner, more legible silhouette edges; garments with excess fabric relative to the body may obscure the underlying silhouette.
observable_patterns: garment fabric lies flat against body with minimal excess
shoulder seam sits at the shoulder crest without overhang
trouser break is minimal or controlled at the shoe
sleeve length ends at the wrist without bunching
no pulling or tension lines visible across fabric
possible_effects: produces a clean, legible outer silhouette edge
reduces visual noise from excess fabric folds
may project a more intentional or structured appearance
allows garment design lines to read as intended
limitations: intentional oversize silhouettes follow different fit logic
fabric weight affects how closely garment hugs the body even when sized correctly
lighting and shadow can create apparent pulling where none exists
pose and movement alter how fit appears in static image
example_claims: Shoulder seam appears aligned at or near the shoulder crest.
No visible tension or pulling lines are detected across the torso fabric.
Garment fabric appears to follow body contours with minimal excess drape.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style
Tan France Queer Eye styling principles

principle: The visual length ratio of the upper garment zone to the lower garment zone influences whether the overall proportional relationship reads as balanced, elongated below, or elongated above.
observable_patterns: cropped top creating a short upper zone with longer lower garment
long tunic or oversized top covering significant lower garment length
high-waisted lower garment raising the apparent waist-to-hem ratio
low-rise lower garment reducing visible lower zone above waistband
untucked shirt extending through the hip zone
possible_effects: cropped upper garment with high-waisted lower piece may increase perceived lower-body length
long upper garment may shorten perceived lower-body zone
high-waisted silhouette may raise apparent waist position
low-rise garment combined with short top creates a proportional midpoint shift
limitations: actual garment boundary may not be visible when layering obscures it
pose and hip tilt affect where apparent waistline falls
camera height and angle distort perceived top-to-bottom ratio
footwear height alters perceived lower-zone length
example_claims: The cropped upper garment creates a visually shorter upper zone relative to the lower garment.
The high-waisted trouser raises the apparent waist position in the silhouette.
The untucked shirt extends into the hip zone, shortening visible lower garment length.

principle: Garment hemlines that terminate near visual thirds of the body—at the hip, mid-thigh, knee, or ankle—interact with perceived proportional divisions in different ways than hemlines falling between these zones.
observable_patterns: jacket hem ending at the hip crest
skirt or short hem falling at mid-thigh
midi hemline landing at or near the knee
maxi hem reaching the ankle or floor
coat hem terminating at mid-calf
possible_effects: hip-length jacket may visually anchor the waist-to-hip zone
mid-thigh hem exposes significant leg length below
knee hemline creates a perceived division at a visual midpoint
floor-length garments create maximal vertical continuity in the lower zone
mid-calf hem may create a horizontal anchor between knee and ankle
limitations: exact hemline position relative to body varies with garment size and individual proportions
footwear choice alters perception of exposed leg length below hem
cropping in image may prevent accurate hemline assessment
fabric weight affects how the hem falls and its apparent position
example_claims: The jacket hem appears to terminate near the hip zone.
The skirt hem falls above the knee, exposing the upper leg zone.
The coat hem appears to end at approximately mid-calf length.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Deliberately oversized garments concentrate visual volume in a zone beyond the body's surface, shifting the silhouette edge outward from the body. Volume placement—upper or lower—affects which zone reads as dominant.
observable_patterns: significant excess fabric visible beyond body edge in torso or sleeve zones
boxy or square outer silhouette with shoulder seam well below shoulder crest
wide-leg or voluminous lower garment with fabric extending well beyond leg edge
oversized shoulder creating an angular, shelf-like silhouette edge
billowing fabric visible in movement even in static image
possible_effects: increases perceived visual mass in the oversized zone
creates a geometric or angular silhouette impression
may reduce visibility of underlying body silhouette
volume concentrated in one zone may create visual counterbalance to another
limitations: intentional oversized styling is context-dependent and culturally coded
fabric stiffness vs drape alters how oversized reads in image
camera angle affects how much excess volume is visible
layering may obscure whether a silhouette is intentionally or accidentally oversized
example_claims: Significant excess fabric is visible in the shoulder and sleeve zone, creating an oversized silhouette.
The boxy outer silhouette edge extends substantially beyond the torso.
Volume appears concentrated in the lower garment, with a narrower upper zone.

principle: Layering pieces introduce additional garment edges and hemlines into the visual field. Where layers align in color or tone, continuity is preserved; where they contrast, they create new segmentation or focal points.
observable_patterns: outer layer in a contrasting color to inner garment
layered hems at different lengths creating visible stacking
matching tonal layers creating a unified outer silhouette
open outer layer revealing inner layer along vertical edges
collar or lapel of inner garment visible above outer layer
possible_effects: contrasting layer adds a new segmentation boundary or focal point
matching tonal layers preserve overall silhouette continuity
visible inner collar or lapel adds a layering detail at the neckline zone
open outer layer creates two additional vertical lines framing the inner garment
limitations: complex layering may create visual noise that resists simple pattern classification
partial visibility of inner layers depends on image crop and pose
fabric texture differences between layers may be invisible in some lighting
movement during capture may obscure layer relationships
example_claims: The outer layer contrasts in color with the inner garment, creating a visible layering boundary.
Matching tonal values between layers preserve vertical continuity.
The open outer layer creates two visible vertical edges framing the inner garment.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: When footwear and lower garment share a close tonal relationship, the lower-body vertical zone appears less interrupted at the hemline, potentially increasing perceived leg length.
observable_patterns: dark trousers paired with dark shoes of similar or identical hue
light-colored lower garment with light or skin-tone footwear
monochromatic lower body styling from waist to foot
no strong tonal contrast visible at the hem-to-shoe transition
possible_effects: may increase perceived vertical length of the lower body zone
reduces visual interruption at the trouser break or hem
may allow the silhouette to read as continuous through the lower zone
shoe becomes less of a focal point when tonally matched
limitations: requires both footwear and lower garment to be visible in the frame
lighting may alter apparent shoe color relative to garment
very different fabrics at matched color may still create visible contrast through texture
sock color visible at the hem gap may reintroduce segmentation
example_claims: Footwear and lower garment share a close tonal value, reducing visible hem-to-shoe contrast.
The lower body zone appears continuous through matched shoe and trouser tones.
No strong tonal break is detected at the hem-to-shoe transition.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Neckline shape and depth create a geometric frame around the face and neck zone that directs visual attention inward or outward, upward or downward.
observable_patterns: deep V-neck creating a downward-pointing triangle
crew or round neck framing the lower face zone
square neckline creating horizontal and vertical edges at the collarbone
off-shoulder or wide neckline emphasizing the horizontal shoulder span
turtleneck or high neck reducing visible neck length
possible_effects: V-neck draws the eye downward toward the chest, may elongate the neck zone visually
crew neck frames the face with a circular border
square neck distributes attention horizontally at the collar region
off-shoulder neckline creates a strong horizontal focal element
turtleneck reduces visible neck, raising the collar frame toward the chin
limitations: hair length and volume may partially obscure neckline geometry
accessories at the neckline compete with or reinforce the neckline focal geometry
image crop may cut off the neckline zone
face direction and chin angle affect how neckline geometry interacts with face framing
example_claims: The V-neckline creates a downward-pointing triangular focal element at the chest.
The square neckline creates a horizontal edge at the collarbone zone.
The turtleneck reduces visible neck length by raising the collar frame.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Visible waist definition—through fit, cinching, belting, or seam placement—creates a narrowing at the midsection that anchors the perceived proportional relationship between upper and lower body zones.
observable_patterns: visible narrowing of fabric at the midsection relative to shoulder and hip zones
belt or cinching element at or near the natural waist
seaming or darting creating an inward curve at the waist region
fabric appears to taper or gather inward at the midsection
possible_effects: may create a visible waist curve in the outer silhouette
anchors a proportional relationship between upper and lower body visual masses
creates a reference point for garment-to-garment proportion reading
belted waist may create a horizontal focal element at the midsection
limitations: loose or untucked garments may obscure any underlying waist shape
pose and body angle affect visibility of waist narrowing
garment structure and fabric stiffness may override waist shaping
image resolution may prevent detection of subtle seaming or darting
example_claims: A visible narrowing at the midsection suggests waist definition in the silhouette.
A belt creates a horizontal focal element at the apparent waist zone.
Fabric appears to taper inward at the midsection through seaming or darting.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: The width of the garment at its lowest visible hem determines the base geometry of the silhouette. Wide hems create A-line or triangular bases; narrow hems create column-like bases.
observable_patterns: flared or A-line skirt with hemline substantially wider than waist zone
straight-cut trouser with consistent width from hip to hem
tapered trouser narrowing toward the ankle
wide-leg trouser maintaining or increasing width through the hem
pencil skirt maintaining narrow width through hemline
possible_effects: A-line hem creates a triangular silhouette with wide base
straight hem produces a columnar or rectangular lower silhouette
tapered hem narrows the base, creating an inverted taper geometry
wide-leg hem maintains horizontal mass through the lower zone
limitations: hem width is only fully readable when full-length image is available
pose with crossed legs or seated position may obscure true hem geometry
fabric movement at wide hems may vary hem width between image captures
shoe choice and stance width interact with apparent hem geometry
example_claims: The flared hemline creates an A-line silhouette base substantially wider than the waist zone.
The tapered trouser narrows toward the ankle, creating a pointed lower silhouette.
The pencil skirt maintains a narrow, columnar geometry through the hem.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Visual weight—determined by color saturation, pattern density, fabric texture, and structural volume—may be perceived as heavier or lighter in different garment zones, influencing overall balance reading.
observable_patterns: dark or saturated color concentrated in the upper body garment
high-contrast pattern or print in the lower garment zone
structured or voluminous upper layer with minimal lower garment
plain upper garment with bold patterned lower garment
heavy texture or embellishment in one zone only
possible_effects: dark or saturated upper zone may appear visually heavier above the waist
bold pattern in lower zone concentrates visual weight below the waist
visual weight above and below in similar quantities may suggest overall balance
heavily textured zone may draw more attention than adjacent plain zones
limitations: visual weight perception is subjective and context-dependent
mixed patterns across zones complicate single-zone weight assessment
image quality and color accuracy affect saturation and pattern density readings
pose and lighting alter how texture and volume read as weight
example_claims: Dark saturated color is concentrated in the upper garment zone.
A bold patterned lower garment creates higher visual weight below the waist.
Structural volume in the upper garment creates greater apparent mass above the waist.

principle: Garment colors that share an undertone (warm or cool) with the wearer's visible skin undertone create tonal harmony; opposing undertones create visible contrast at the skin-garment boundary.
observable_patterns: yellow, orange, or red-adjacent hues in garments near the face
blue, green, or purple-adjacent hues in garments near the face
visible skin at neckline, face, or hands for undertone reference
golden or peachy skin tones adjacent to garment colors at collar
pink or bluish skin tones adjacent to garment colors at collar
possible_effects: warm garment adjacent to warm undertone skin may create a harmonious transition
cool garment adjacent to cool undertone skin may appear to recede or blend
opposing undertone combinations may create heightened contrast at the skin-garment boundary
high undertone contrast near the face may increase or decrease perceived skin clarity in the image
limitations: undertone assessment from images is highly sensitive to lighting color temperature
camera white balance settings significantly alter apparent skin and garment undertones
image compression artifacts may distort color values
the 12-season system has no universal consensus on classification boundaries
example_claims: The garment at the neckline contains warm yellow-adjacent hues adjacent to the visible skin zone.
A cool blue-toned garment is observed adjacent to the visible skin at the collar.
Garment and visible skin hues appear to share a similar warm undertone register.

principle: A region of high tonal or chromatic contrast within an outfit draws disproportionate visual attention relative to surrounding lower-contrast zones.
observable_patterns: bright or saturated accessory against a dark neutral garment
white shirt collar visible above a dark jacket
high-contrast patterned zone surrounded by solid-color zones
strongly contrasting belt against both upper and lower garment colors
bright shoes against dark trousers
possible_effects: high-contrast element becomes a focal point in the outfit composition
draws the eye to the zone where contrast is highest
may reduce attention to other outfit zones by comparison
a single high-contrast accessory can anchor the overall outfit narrative
limitations: multiple high-contrast elements may distribute attention and reduce individual focal emphasis
image exposure settings alter apparent contrast levels
background contrast may compete with outfit-internal contrast
small area of high contrast may be insufficient to dominate in a busy composition
example_claims: A high-contrast bright element is present against a dark background garment, likely creating a focal point.
The white collar creates strong tonal contrast against the dark jacket above it.
Saturated footwear creates a high-contrast element at the base of a low-contrast outfit.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Outfits constructed from colors of similar tonal value (all light, all medium, or all dark) produce a low-contrast ensemble where individual elements recede into a unified visual field.
observable_patterns: multiple garments of similar lightness regardless of hue
all garments within 2-3 tonal steps on a lightness scale
no single element substantially lighter or darker than surrounding pieces
neutrals of similar tone combined without clear contrast element
possible_effects: creates a cohesive, unified visual impression
reduces visual fragmentation between garment pieces
may reduce the appearance of individual garments as separate elements
produces a quieter, more restrained visual register
limitations: tonal similarity is relative and depends on accurate color reproduction in image
hue differences may create perceived contrast even at similar lightness levels
texture differences between tonally matched pieces may reintroduce visible contrast
high ambient lighting may wash out tonal distinctions in the image
example_claims: All visible garments fall within a similar tonal range, creating a low-contrast ensemble.
No single element appears substantially lighter or darker than the surrounding pieces.
The outfit reads as a unified tonal field rather than as distinct contrasting elements.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Garment pieces in hues positioned opposite each other on the color wheel create chromatic tension that draws the eye to the boundary where they meet.
observable_patterns: red and green garments appearing in the same outfit
blue and orange elements combined in one ensemble
purple and yellow combination in garment pieces
complementary hue combination in garment and accessory
possible_effects: creates vibrancy and visual tension at the color boundary
each color may appear more saturated when adjacent to its complement
the boundary zone becomes a focal element
can create a high-energy visual register depending on saturation levels
limitations: muted or desaturated complements produce much lower tension than saturated ones
exact hue complement relationships vary across color spaces
image processing and monitor calibration affect apparent complementary relationships
large area ratios between complements alter perceived balance significantly
example_claims: The garment combination includes hues that appear to sit in a complementary relationship on the color wheel.
Adjacent complementary hues create a visible chromatic boundary in the outfit.
Color tension is observable between the two dominant garment hues.

principle: Combining garment pieces in hues adjacent to each other on the color wheel produces tonal harmony with low chromatic tension.
observable_patterns: outfit containing adjacent hues such as blue, blue-green, and green
warm family ensemble using orange, coral, and yellow
cool family ensemble combining navy, teal, and sage
adjacent hues across multiple garment pieces without a strongly contrasting color
possible_effects: creates a harmonious, cohesive color impression
reduces chromatic tension between garment pieces
ensemble may read as color-coordinated rather than color-contrasted
produces a sophisticated or restrained visual register depending on saturation
limitations: analogous range is not universally defined and varies across color theory traditions
image color accuracy depends on lighting and camera calibration
very different saturation levels between adjacent hues can reintroduce contrast
hue distance within the analogous range affects the degree of harmony
example_claims: Garment hues appear to fall within an adjacent range on the color wheel.
The ensemble reads as tonally harmonious with limited chromatic contrast.
Warm adjacent hues are observable across multiple garment pieces.

principle: Garments in neutral hues (black, white, navy, grey, camel, beige, olive) create low chromatic contrast ensembles with high compatibility across different styling contexts.
observable_patterns: all garment pieces in achromatic or near-neutral hues
black and white combination with no chromatic accent
navy and camel combination
grey and white ensemble
head-to-toe earth tone combination
possible_effects: creates a visually restrained and cohesive ensemble
neutral pieces pair more easily with a wider range of other garments
reduces chromatic complexity, allowing silhouette and structure to dominate
may project a formal, classic, or understated register
limitations: neutral classification varies across color traditions
some so-called neutrals (camel, olive) carry warm or cool undertone signals
all-neutral ensembles may read as visually flat in low-contrast image conditions
neutral does not imply tonally matched, and tonal contrast within neutrals is still present
example_claims: All visible garment pieces fall within a neutral or achromatic hue range.
No chromatic accent color is visible in the outfit composition.
The ensemble reads as tonally restrained using neutrals throughout.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: A single saturated or chromatic accent color introduced into an otherwise neutral or low-saturation ensemble creates a focal element at the location of the accent.
observable_patterns: one saturated colored accessory against an otherwise neutral outfit
single colored shoe against monochromatic or neutral ensemble
bright handbag or bag element as only non-neutral in frame
colored belt or scarf as sole accent element
one colored garment piece surrounded by neutrals
possible_effects: accent becomes the dominant focal point in the outfit composition
draws the eye to the zone where the accent is placed
increases perceived sophistication of neutral base through controlled use of color
creates a narrative element in an otherwise understated ensemble
limitations: accent power depends on the degree of saturation contrast with surrounding elements
multiple accents reduce individual focal emphasis
ambient light and image saturation settings affect how an accent reads
small accents may not be visible or dominant in full-length image frames
example_claims: A single saturated accent element appears against an otherwise neutral outfit palette.
The colored accessory creates a focal point in the otherwise low-saturation ensemble.
Only one chromatic element is visible in the outfit composition, concentrating visual attention.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: The overall saturation level of an outfit—whether composed of bright, vivid hues versus muted, greyed hues—correlates with the perceived visual energy or quietness of the ensemble.
observable_patterns: multiple highly saturated garment pieces visible in the outfit
all garments in heavily muted, greyed, or dusty tones
single vivid piece contrasting against heavily muted surrounding garments
consistent mid-saturation across all garment pieces
possible_effects: high saturation across multiple pieces may increase perceived visual energy
muted, dusty palette may produce a quieter, more restrained visual register
mixed saturation levels create visual tension between vivid and muted elements
consistent saturation level produces a harmonious if potentially monotone visual impression
limitations: saturation perception is highly sensitive to image exposure and processing
hue identity may be lost at very low saturation levels, making color classification unreliable
cultural associations of specific saturations vary widely
garment material reflectivity affects apparent saturation in image
example_claims: The visible garments appear in highly saturated hues, creating a high-energy color composition.
All garment pieces appear in muted, low-saturation tones.
Mixed saturation levels are observed across the outfit components.

principle: Garments with blue-toned, icy, or ash-toned hues correspond to the cool end of seasonal palette systems, producing specific chromatic qualities observable in the image.
observable_patterns: icy pastels with blue or lavender undertone
true blue or blue-grey garment tones
ash-toned brown or grey without warm golden cast
cool red or burgundy without orange undertone
stark black and white contrast without warm cream
possible_effects: cool-toned palette creates a crisp, cool visual register
icy pastels at the face may contrast with warm skin undertones
deep cool tones may create sharper apparent contrast at skin boundaries
cool neutrals may recede visually relative to warm ones in similar lighting
limitations: cool vs warm palette assessment is highly vulnerable to lighting color temperature shifts
image compression and camera sensor characteristics distort hue accuracy
the 12-season system produces varying classifications across practitioners
individual undertone assessment from image alone is unreliable without controlled conditions
example_claims: The visible garment hues appear to fall within a cool or ash-toned palette range.
Blue or blue-adjacent undertones are observable in the garment colors.
No warm golden or yellow undertones are visible in the garment palette.

principle: Garments with golden, earthy, orange-adjacent, or warm-toned hues correspond to the warm end of seasonal palette systems, producing observable chromatic warmth in the image.
observable_patterns: camel, rust, terracotta, or mustard garment tones
warm brown, warm green, or olive tones
cream or off-white with yellow or warm undertone
orange-red rather than blue-red hues
warm peach or coral tones
possible_effects: warm-toned palette creates a golden, earthy visual register
warm hues adjacent to warm undertone skin may create a harmonious skin-garment transition
earth tones may produce a grounded, autumnal visual impression
warm neutrals tend to recede less aggressively than cool neutrals in certain lighting
limitations: warm vs cool classification depends on accurate color rendering in the image
yellow-toned lighting can warm any garment regardless of its actual hue
warm colors appear to advance in visual space under neutral lighting, potentially increasing visual weight
the boundaries between warm and cool are not universally fixed in seasonal systems
example_claims: The visible garment hues appear to fall within a warm, earth-toned palette range.
Golden or orange-adjacent undertones are observable in the garment colors.
No cool blue or ash undertones are visible in the dominant garment palette.

principle: The scale of a print or pattern relative to the garment area and the wearer's apparent proportions affects whether the print reads as large or small, and how it interacts with perceived visual mass.
observable_patterns: small repeat pattern on a large garment area
large-scale print on a small garment zone
print scale similar to or larger than visible body zone
micro print creating a near-solid visual texture from viewing distance
bold oversized print dominant in the frame
possible_effects: large print relative to garment area may increase apparent visual mass of that garment
small repeat pattern reads as texture rather than pattern at distance
oversized print may draw strong focal attention
micro print creates a textured solid rather than a pattern, reducing focal complexity
limitations: print scale-to-figure reading depends on image distance and resolution
image compression may obscure fine print details
print design complexity independently affects visual weight beyond simple scale
print placement within a garment panel affects which zones receive emphasis
example_claims: A large-scale print is visible across the upper garment zone, creating a dominant focal pattern.
The print appears as a fine texture rather than a readable pattern at the image resolution.
Print scale appears large relative to the visible garment area.

principle: Fabrics with high drape—such as silk, chiffon, viscose, or fluid jersey—fall away from the body, creating soft, curved silhouette edges that follow gravity rather than holding structural shape.
observable_patterns: fabric folds falling vertically along the body with gravity
curved rather than angular hem edges
fabric visible to shift or pool at hemline
garment surface showing fluid curves rather than flat planes
multiple overlapping folds or bias drape visible
possible_effects: creates a soft, fluid silhouette edge that shifts with movement
reduces structural rigidity in the garment's outer contour
draping folds may create visual movement in a static image
high drape reduces visible crease lines compared to stiffer fabrics
limitations: drape behavior is affected by garment cut, not just fabric type
body position and pose alter drape direction significantly
fabric movement during capture may create unusual drape patterns
image resolution may not resolve fine drape folds
example_claims: The garment fabric falls in soft vertical folds consistent with high-drape material behavior.
The silhouette edge appears soft and curved rather than angular or stiff.
Fluid drape folds are visible across the skirt or garment body.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Fabrics with low drape—such as canvas, denim, tweed, structured wool, or crisp cotton—hold their shape away from the body, projecting a defined silhouette edge with visible form retention.
observable_patterns: garment maintains angular or geometric edges at shoulders or hem
fabric surface appears flat with minimal fold variation
collar, lapel, or jacket front maintains a rigid position
structured waistband or waist region maintains visible shape
sleeves or trouser legs maintain consistent shape independent of body position
possible_effects: creates a structured, architectural silhouette impression
maintains garment design lines more visibly than drape-heavy fabrics
suggests formality or tailoring through visual rigidity
stiff fabric may resist body contour, reducing visible body-line definition
limitations: fabric stiffness cannot be definitively identified from visual cues alone
structured tailoring techniques can replicate stiffness with moderately drapey fabrics using interfacing
garment pressing and steaming affects apparent crispness in a still image
worn or washed stiff fabrics may lose visible structural rigidity
example_claims: The garment maintains a structured shape with angular edges consistent with a stiff fabric.
The collar and lapels appear rigid, holding their position without draping.
The trouser leg maintains a consistent silhouette shape through the image.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Tension lines or fabric pulling across a garment indicate that the garment is under stress, typically signaling that the garment is smaller than the body dimensions in that zone.
observable_patterns: diagonal pulling lines visible across the chest or back
button gap visible at the shirt or jacket closure
fabric appearing stretched or distorted across a joint or curved body region
horizontal pulling lines across the thigh or hip zone
trouser seat pulling with visible fabric stress
possible_effects: suggests the garment is smaller than the body in the zone showing tension
may indicate fit mismatch between garment size and body dimension
can create distortion of intended design lines in the tension zone
may draw visual attention to the area of tension through visible fabric stress
limitations: some garments are intentionally stretch-fit and tension lines may be expected

principle: Excess fabric visible in a zone where the garment extends substantially beyond the body's surface indicates that the garment is larger than the body dimension in that zone—unless the design is intentionally oversized.
observable_patterns: fabric bunching or pooling at the waist or hip area in a trouser
shirt or jacket hanging significantly away from the torso
sleeve length extending well beyond the wrist
trouser break with significant fabric stacking at the shoe
collar gaping away from the neck on a tailored garment

can create a looseness or shapelessness impression in non-oversized styling contexts

limitations: intentional oversized garments are a deliberate design choice and excess fabric is expected
fabric type affects how pooling or bunching reads—heavier fabrics fall differently
pose and arm position affect sleeve excess visibility
image cropping may prevent assessment of trouser break or shoe interaction

source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: The position of the shoulder seam relative to the shoulder crest is a key tailoring fit indicator. A seam at or near the shoulder crest indicates an approximate fit; seams significantly inward or outward suggest size mismatch or intentional design.
observable_patterns: shoulder seam visually aligned at the highest point of the shoulder
seam extending past the shoulder crest onto the upper arm
seam falling significantly inward toward the neck on a non-drop-shoulder garment
seam creates a shelf or overhang past the shoulder point
possible_effects: seam at crest suggests the garment shoulder width is aligned with the wearer
seam past the crest onto the arm suggests the garment is wider than the wearer at the shoulder
dropped shoulder seams are a design feature creating a specific silhouette effect
seam inward of crest suggests garment is narrower than the wearer at the shoulder
limitations: shoulder crest position varies with pose, arm position, and clothing bulk
dropped shoulder is an intentional design in many contemporary garments
image angle may obscure the seam-to-crest relationship
layering may shift the apparent position of the outer shoulder seam

The shoulder seam appears inward of the shoulder crest on a structured jacket.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: The amount of trouser fabric that contacts or stacks on the shoe at the hem determines the trouser break—a tailoring measurement with a spectrum from no-break (full hem above shoe) to full break (fabric stacking significantly).

fabric stacks or bunches above the shoe in multiple folds

full break creates a heavier, pooling hem impression
excess break may read as accidental length or size mismatch in formal contexts
limitations: full-length image required to assess trouser break
shoe heel height affects apparent break at the same trouser length
trouser break conventions vary by regional tailoring tradition and era
image perspective and angle distort apparent hem-to-shoe relationship

principle: Combining garment pieces with contrasting surface textures—such as smooth and rough, matte and shiny, ribbed and flat—creates visible surface variation that may add visual interest without chromatic contrast.

fashion trends adapt collar formality conventions over time
unbuttoned collar may be intentional styling choice rather than fit issue
example_claims: A structured spread collar dress shirt is visible, consistent with business formal formality indicators.
A collarless top is visible, reducing collar-zone formality signals.
The shirt collar appears open and unbuttoned, shifting the visible formality register.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Visible garment condition—including visible wrinkles, pilling, fading, distressing, or structural damage—affects the perceived care level and intentionality of a garment in the context of its occasion register.
observable_patterns: visible heavy creasing or wrinkling across garment surface
fabric pilling visible on knitwear or wool surfaces
visible fading or color inconsistency on denim
intentional distressing such as rips, fraying, or whiskering on denim
staining, snags, or structural damage visible on garment
possible_effects: heavy wrinkling in a formal garment may suggest wear or care misalignment
intentional distressing signals a deliberate casual or street styling choice
pilling or fading may reduce perceived garment quality independent of cut or design
pristine condition across all garments signals care investment
limitations: some wrinkles are inherent to fabric type and do not indicate neglect
intentional vs accidental distressing requires styling context to distinguish
image resolution and lighting affect how fabric condition reads
shadow patterns may appear as wrinkles or damage in some lighting conditions
example_claims: Visible heavy creasing is present across the garment surface.
Intentional distressing is visible in the denim garment consistent with casual or street styling.
Fabric pilling is observable on the knitwear garment surface.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Fabric weight interacts with garment silhouette to determine whether a garment holds a structured shape or collapses into a draped form. Heavier fabrics tend to fall with more gravity and structure; lighter fabrics may float or gather.
observable_patterns: thick or substantial-appearing fabric maintaining a rigid hem or edge
lightweight fabric appearing to float or billow even in a static image
coat or outerwear fabric appearing to hold its form away from the body
thin or sheer fabric gathering or folding with gravity
medium-weight fabric falling with a defined drape without extreme rigidity
possible_effects: heavy fabric creates a grounded, structured garment impression
lightweight fabric creates a floating, ethereal garment impression
medium-weight fabric balances structure and drape for a versatile visual effect
fabric weight affects how clearly design lines and silhouette edges read
limitations: fabric weight is not directly observable and must be inferred from drape and structural behavior
garment construction and lining may create heavier behavior from a lighter base fabric
image still captures may not reveal fabric weight-dependent movement or float
environmental conditions such as wind affect how lightweight fabrics appear
example_claims: The garment fabric appears to maintain a structured form consistent with a heavier weight material.
The garment fabric appears to float or billow consistent with a lightweight material.
The coat fabric appears to hold its form at the hem, suggesting structural weight.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Sheer or semi-transparent fabrics create visible layering depth by revealing underlying garment layers or body contour. The degree of transparency determines how much visual information passes through.
observable_patterns: inner garment or lining visible through outer sheer layer
skin tone or body contour visible through semi-transparent fabric
fabric color appearing lighter or less saturated over dark underlayer
pattern on inner garment partially visible through sheer overlay
sheer sleeves or panels visible in combination with opaque garment zones
possible_effects: sheer layering creates visible depth and dimensionality in the garment zone
inner contrast layer visible through sheer creates a focal element
partial transparency allows body contour information to remain visible
sheer zones adjacent to opaque create contrast in visual information density
limitations: degree of transparency depends on fabric construction and cannot be precisely quantified from image
backlighting significantly increases apparent transparency
compressed images may lose transparency detail in sheer zones
styling intent of sheer layers is not assessable from image alone
example_claims: An inner garment is partially visible through a sheer outer layer.
Skin tone is partially visible through a semi-transparent fabric panel.
Sheer sleeves create a visible contrast in opacity with adjacent opaque garment zones.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Embellishment—including sequins, embroidery, beading, or hardware—creates areas of concentrated visual complexity that function as focal elements within an outfit.
observable_patterns: sequin or metallic embellishment visible on a garment surface
embroidery pattern concentrated in a specific garment zone
beading or crystal detailing visible at collar, hem, or sleeve
studded or hardware-detailed zone on jacket or belt
embellished pocket or panel zone surrounded by plain fabric
possible_effects: embellished zone becomes a dominant focal point in the outfit composition
reflective embellishment may increase visual weight in the embellished zone
concentrated embellishment surrounded by plain zones creates a strong focal-to-rest contrast
embellishment at specific garment zones directs attention to those locations
limitations: fine embellishment may not be visible at standard image resolution
lighting direction is critical for embellishment visibility, especially reflective types
embellishment density and distribution affect whether a single or multiple focal zones exist
embellishment on a heavily patterned garment may reduce rather than increase focal clarity
example_claims: Sequin or metallic embellishment is visible concentrated in the upper garment zone, creating a focal element.
Embroidery detailing is visible at the collar, directing attention to the neckline zone.
Studded hardware creates a focal element at the belt or jacket zone.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Footwear heel height alters the visible alignment of the lower leg relative to the floor, affecting the perceived proportion of the lower body zone and its interaction with trouser break, hem length, and overall silhouette height.
observable_patterns: flat shoe or sneaker visible with low or no heel
block heel or kitten heel visible with moderate elevation
stiletto or high heel visible with significant elevation
platform sole visible with significant but different elevation geometry than thin heel
trouser break or skirt hem relationship to shoe height visible
possible_effects: higher heel raises the foot arch and may alter the visual weight line of the lower leg
heel height changes how trouser break quantity reads at a given trouser length
flat footwear combined with hemlines calibrated for heels may create excess trouser break
platform soles create elevation without ankle angle change, producing a different silhouette effect from thin heels
limitations: full-length image required to assess heel height and its relationship with hemline
heel height assessment from image is approximate
trouser break and hemline measurement requires the full leg zone to be visible
pose angle and foot position affect how heel height reads in image
example_claims: Flat footwear is visible, suggesting no heel-height elevation effect on the lower silhouette.
High heels are visible, which may interact with the trouser break length at the hem.
Platform soles are visible, creating vertical elevation with a different geometry from stiletto heels.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Sleeve length is a formality and proportion signal. Full-length sleeves are associated with higher formality; shorter or absent sleeves carry lower formality associations that interact with other garment elements.
observable_patterns: long sleeve ending at or near the wrist visible
three-quarter sleeve ending between elbow and wrist visible
short sleeve ending at or near the mid-upper arm visible
sleeveless garment with no sleeve visible
sleeve rolled up revealing a portion of the forearm
possible_effects: full-length sleeve suggests a more formal or conservative visual register
sleeveless upper garment may reduce perceived formality unless compensated by other elements
rolled sleeve may suggest a relaxed or informal modifier to an otherwise structured garment
three-quarter sleeve occupies an intermediate proportion zone
limitations: sleeve length interacts with garment type in determining formality—a sleeveless blazer reads differently from a sleeveless t-shirt
layering may add or obscure sleeve length
image crop may prevent sleeve length assessment
pose and arm position alter the visible sleeve length in an image
example_claims: A full-length sleeve ending at the wrist is visible, consistent with a more formal register signal.
A sleeveless upper garment is visible, reducing sleeve-zone formality signals.
A rolled sleeve is visible, suggesting an informal modifier to the garment register.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Repeating a color across multiple garment zones or accessories creates a visual echo that increases the perceived cohesion of the outfit composition.
observable_patterns: same hue appearing in both garment and accessory
shoe and belt or bag visible in the same color
accent hue from garment print echoed in a solid accessory
inner layer color echoed in an outer layer trim or detail
hat or headwear repeating a garment color
possible_effects: color repetition creates a visual linking between garment zones and accessories
echoed colors increase perceived intentionality of the outfit combination
distributed repetition of one hue may create a coherent color narrative across the outfit
single-zone accent hue echoed in an accessory reinforces the accent's role in the palette
limitations: color repetition requires accurate color comparison across different garment types and textures
lighting variation across zones of the image may make apparent color matching unreliable
subtle color echoes may not be detectable at standard image resolution
image color calibration affects cross-zone color comparison accuracy
example_claims: The shoe and belt appear in a matching or closely related color, creating a visual echo.
An accent hue from the garment appears to be repeated in a visible accessory.
The same color is observed in both a garment detail and a standalone accessory.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Preppy style is associated with observable signals drawn from Ivy League and country club fashion traditions: specific garment types, heritage palette, polo or oxford shirts, nautical elements, and clean, structured silhouettes.
observable_patterns: polo shirt, oxford shirt, or button-down shirt visible
chino trouser, madras, or plaid short visible
navy, cream, kelly green, red, or yellow palette dominant
boat shoe, loafer, or clean sneaker visible as footwear
cable-knit or fine knitwear visible
blazer or structured jacket in heritage palette
possible_effects: cluster of preppy indicators suggests alignment with the preppy style archetype
heritage palette and garment type combination create a traditional American collegiate register
individual preppy garment types placed in other contexts may signal hybrid or ironic styling
limitations: preppy archetype overlaps significantly with classic and old money archetypes
individual preppy garment types appear in many non-preppy style contexts
geographical and generational variation in preppy conventions is significant
brand identification may be required to confirm heritage vs generic interpretation
example_claims: A polo shirt and chino combination in a heritage palette is visible, consistent with preppy style signals.
Boat shoe or loafer footwear is visible alongside a blazer in a navy palette.
Multiple preppy garment types are present with a classic heritage color palette.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style

principle: Utilitarian style draws on workwear and functional clothing traditions, characterized by observable garment features such as cargo pockets, durable fabric appearances, neutral or military-adjacent palette, and functional design elements.
observable_patterns: cargo pockets visible on trousers or jacket
canvas, denim, or heavy cotton fabric appearance
military olive, tan, khaki, or navy palette dominant
work boot or trail boot visible as footwear
technical or functional-appearing garment detailing
utility vest or jacket with multiple pockets visible
possible_effects: cluster of utilitarian indicators suggests alignment with the utilitarian style archetype
functional design elements in a non-functional context signal deliberate utilitarian styling
military-adjacent palette combined with workwear garment types reinforces utilitarian register
limitations: utilitarian garment types appear widely in non-utilitarian contexts through fashion adoption
luxury brands produce utilitarian-coded garments with different quality and price signals
individual functional elements in otherwise non-utilitarian outfits create hybrid readings
the distinction between utilitarian styling and workwear is context-dependent
example_claims: Cargo trousers with multiple visible pockets are present in an olive or khaki palette.
Heavy canvas or work boot footwear is visible alongside functional garment detailing.
Multiple utilitarian garment elements are visible in a military-adjacent color palette.

principle: Avant-garde style is characterized by observable features that depart from conventional garment construction and proportion norms: asymmetry, deconstruction, extreme proportion, unexpected material use, or deliberate subversion of standard garment forms.
observable_patterns: asymmetric hemline or construction visible
garment appears deconstructed with exposed seams or raw edges
extreme proportion—very oversized or very undersized relative to conventional fit
unexpected material use visible such as structured sculptural volumes
garment form does not correspond to conventional garment categories
deliberate subversion of expected garment function or structure
possible_effects: avant-garde indicators signal a departure from conventional styling conventions
deconstruction and asymmetry draw attention to garment construction as a design element
extreme proportion may create unfamiliar silhouette readings outside typical fashion grammar
unexpected material use may create confusion with standard garment classification
limitations: avant-garde assessment is highly contextually dependent
contemporary fashion has adopted many formerly avant-garde elements into mainstream contexts
deconstruction as a design choice vs wear damage requires contextual judgment
image framing and resolution affect how avant-garde construction details read
example_claims: An asymmetric hemline is visible, departing from conventional garment hem geometry.
Exposed seams or raw edge finishing is visible, consistent with deconstructed design.
The garment silhouette appears to exceed conventional proportion norms significantly.

principle: Garments that carry visible quality signals—construction precision, fabric texture integrity, structural consistency, minimal wear indicators—may proxy for higher durability and wear frequency potential, supporting cost-per-wear logic.
observable_patterns: seams and stitching appear precise and even at visible edges
fabric surface appears consistent without pilling, snags, or distortion
garment structure maintains its shape under normal wear conditions
finishing details such as hem, collar, or pocket edges appear clean
no visible stress or wear at high-friction zones
possible_effects: visible quality construction signals may proxy for garment durability
structurally consistent garments appear well-maintained relative to usage level
quality signals support wardrobe logic of higher initial investment for longer wear
limitations: garment quality from image alone is highly unreliable—price, brand, and material cannot be confirmed visually
new garments of all quality levels may appear pristine in images
photographic resolution may not resolve fine construction quality details
stylistic manipulation such as pressing or steaming affects apparent quality signals in images
example_claims: Visible seams and finishing appear clean and precise at the observable edges.
The garment surface appears consistent without visible pilling or distortion.
No visible stress or wear is detected at the collar, cuffs, or pocket zones.
source_refs: Tim Gunn - A Guide to Quality Taste and Style
